behaviors.description: SUM(A1:A10) evaluates to the correct sum of values in the referenced range.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is entirely within the SUM function's range-expansion and accumulation logic. No module handoff is required — given a cell grid (or stub), the SUM evaluator either accumulates correctly or it doesn't. REAL-INFRA: No I/O. LLM-DEP: No AI. The bug (off-by-one in range bounds, wrong accumulation) lives inside one algorithmic boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Construct a minimal in-memory cell grid with known values in A1:A10. Invoke the formula evaluator on `=SUM(A1:A10)` and assert the result equals the expected sum. Also test edge cases: all zeros, negative values, mixed positive/negative, and a range where some cells are empty (should treat as 0).

behaviors.description: A formula referencing another formula cell (e.g., =B1+1 where B1=A1*2) produces the correct cascaded result.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure only manifests when the evaluator must request B1's value, which triggers evaluation of B1's own formula, which then fetches A1. This is a multi-module handoff: formula parser → dependency resolver → evaluator (recursively). Mocking the cell-value fetch always passes because the bug lives in the wiring between the resolver and the evaluator re-entry. A single-function unit test cannot expose this. REAL-INFRA/LLM-DEP: Neither applies.
behaviors.test_description: Wire up a small in-memory workbook with A1=5, B1 formula `=A1*2`, and a target cell formula `=B1+1`. Evaluate the target cell through the full engine pipeline and assert the result is 11. Also set A1=0 and re-evaluate to confirm cascading recalculation propagates correctly.

behaviors.description: The engine detects a circular dependency (A1→B1→A1) and returns a circular reference error instead of looping.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Cycle detection requires the formula parser to build the dependency graph AND the dependency resolver to traverse it AND the evaluator to abort on a detected cycle rather than stack-overflow or infinite-loop. Each of those three modules must hand off correctly; a unit test of the cycle-detection algorithm in isolation (on a pre-built graph) cannot catch a wiring bug where the evaluator never consults the cycle detector. REAL-INFRA/LLM-DEP: Neither applies.
behaviors.test_description: Create an in-memory workbook where A1's formula is `=B1` and B1's formula is `=A1`. Invoke the engine evaluator on A1 and assert it returns a circular-reference error type (not an exception, not a value). Also test a 3-node cycle (A→B→C→A) to ensure detection is not limited to 2-node loops. Assert non-circular references are not flagged.

behaviors.description: VLOOKUP with exact match returns the correct value and returns an error when the key is not found.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: VLOOKUP's lookup and exact-match logic is self-contained within the function implementation. Given a table (as an in-memory range), it either matches the key or it doesn't. The failure modes (returning wrong row, wrong column offset, missing error on not-found) all live inside one function boundary. No multi-module handoff is required to expose these bugs. REAL-INFRA/LLM-DEP: Neither applies.
behaviors.test_description: Construct an in-memory lookup table (3 columns, 5 rows). Call VLOOKUP with a key that exists and assert the correct column value is returned. Call VLOOKUP with a key that does not exist and assert a #N/A or equivalent not-found error is returned. Also test boundary keys (first row, last row) and a table with duplicate keys (should return first match).

behaviors.description: The IF function correctly handles nested conditions (e.g., =IF(A1>10, IF(A1>20, "high", "medium"), "low")).
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Nested IF evaluation is purely an expression-parsing and recursive-evaluation problem. The failure modes (wrong branch taken, incorrect short-circuit, wrong AST nesting) live entirely within the expression evaluator for a single formula string. A unit test can supply the cell value directly and verify which branch is selected. No module handoff is required. REAL-INFRA/LLM-DEP: Neither applies.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the evaluator with A1 set to each of three representative values (5, 15, 25). Evaluate `=IF(A1>10, IF(A1>20, "high", "medium"), "low")` for each and assert the results are "low", "medium", and "high" respectively. Also test boundary values (exactly 10 and exactly 20) to confirm comparison operators are not off-by-one.

behaviors.description: CONCATENATE joins cell values with the correct separator and skips empty cells rather than inserting blank strings.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The empty-cell-skipping behavior is a policy decision inside CONCATENATE's implementation. The failure (inserting an empty string rather than skipping) lives in one function's argument-filtering logic. No multi-module handoff is involved. A unit test can supply a mix of non-empty and empty cell values directly. REAL-INFRA/LLM-DEP: Neither applies.
behaviors.test_description: Call CONCATENATE with a mix of non-empty strings and empty cells (e.g., ["Hello", "", "World"]). Assert the result is "HelloWorld" (empty skipped, no extra separator artifact). Also test all-empty inputs (expect empty string or defined behavior), a single non-empty value (no separator added), and verify the separator character is applied only between non-empty values.

behaviors.description: A formula referencing a cell in a different sheet (e.g., =Sheet2!A1+1) resolves correctly when the referenced sheet exists.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure here occurs at the boundary between the formula parser (which must recognize the `Sheet2!A1` syntax) and the sheet-registry/workbook module (which must locate Sheet2 and fetch A1's value). A unit test of the parser alone or the registry alone cannot expose a wiring bug where the evaluated sheet-qualified reference is passed in the wrong form to the registry lookup. The bug surfaces only when both modules are connected. REAL-INFRA/LLM-DEP: Neither applies.
behaviors.test_description: Create an in-memory workbook with two sheets. Set Sheet2!A1 to a known value (e.g., 10). Evaluate `=Sheet2!A1+1` from a cell on Sheet1 and assert the result is 11. Also test that referencing a sheet that does not exist returns a #REF! error, and that changing Sheet2!A1 and re-evaluating produces the updated result.

behaviors.description: When a referenced cell's value changes, all dependent formulas are recalculated in the correct topological order.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Correct topological recalculation requires the dependency graph module, the change-notification dispatcher, and the evaluator to interact in the right order. A bug where a cell is recalculated before its dependency is updated (wrong topological order) is invisible unless all three modules are wired together and a chain of at least three cells is exercised. Unit tests of each component separately cannot expose ordering violations. REAL-INFRA/LLM-DEP: Neither applies.
behaviors.test_description: Build a three-cell chain in-memory: A1=literal 2, B1=`=A1*3`, C1=`=B1+1`. Assert initial values. Then update A1 to 4 and trigger recalculation. Assert B1=12 and C1=13 in the correct order (B1 must be recalculated before C1). Also build a diamond dependency (A1→B1, A1→C1, B1 and C1 both→D1) and verify D1 is recalculated exactly once after A1 changes.

behaviors.description: The undo operation after editing a formula restores both the formula text and all previously computed dependent values.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Undo correctness requires the undo/history module to capture a snapshot of both the formula string and the computed dependent values, and the formula engine to restore that snapshot AND re-run recalculation (or restore cached values) so dependents reflect the prior state. The failure (formula restored but dependents stale, or vice versa) only surfaces when the undo system and the recalculation engine are exercised together. REAL-INFRA/LLM-DEP: Neither applies.
behaviors.test_description: Set up A1=5, B1=`=A1*2` (computed=10), C1=`=B1+1` (computed=11). Edit B1's formula to `=A1*3` (C1 becomes 16). Execute undo. Assert B1's formula is `=A1*2`, B1's computed value is 10, and C1's computed value is 11. Also verify that redo after undo restores the edited state correctly.

behaviors.description: Saving a workbook to disk and loading it back produces identical cell values and formulas with no data loss or floating-point precision change.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The serializer and deserializer must agree on the binary encoding of formulas, cell values, and floating-point representation. A bug (precision loss, formula string truncation, sheet name encoding) surfaces only in the actual byte round-trip. REAL-INFRA: This requires real filesystem writes (to a temp file) to exercise the actual I/O path; an in-memory byte buffer integration test is sufficient and avoids requiring OS-specific paths or elevated permissions — not a subprocess or external service. LLM-DEP: No AI.
behaviors.test_description: Create a workbook with: a literal integer cell, a literal float cell (e.g., 1.0/3.0 to stress precision), a formula cell, and a cross-sheet formula cell. Serialize to a temporary file on disk. Deserialize from that temp file into a new workbook object. Assert every cell's formula string is identical, every numeric value is bit-for-bit equal (or within documented precision tolerance), and no cells are missing. Clean up the temp file after the test.
